original_thesis: The applied probability stack. Expected value frames decisions; Monte Carlo simulates outcomes; regression-to-the-mean calibrates expectations; conjunction fallacy catches compound-probability errors.
corrected_thesis: The applied probability stack. Probabilistic thinking provides the foundational frame (degrees of belief, not binary certainty); expected value quantifies decisions under uncertainty; regression-to-the-mean calibrates expectations after extreme observations; conjunction fallacy catches compound-probability errors (people overestimate the probability of multiple conditions being met); systems thinking reveals how probabilistic outcomes cascade through interconnected systems.
what_this_stack_defeats: Deterministic thinking, narrative probability, ignoring reversion, overestimating conjunctive events
notes: Original thesis mentioned Monte Carlo — not a member. Minor correction. The actual members form a coherent 'think in probabilities' stack distinct from Quantitative Calibration (which is the 'calibrate with data' stack).
